title: Floquet Engineering of Quantum Transport through two Driven Impurities
authors: Vincenzo Bruno, Corinna Kollath, Roberta Citro, Ameneh Sheikhan
abstract: Floquet engineering offers powerful tools to manipulate quantum states by periodically driving physical parameters. In this work, we investigate the quantum transport through two periodically driven impurities in a mesoscopic one-dimensional channel. By mapping the time-dependent Hamiltonian into an effective multichannel scattering problem, we unveil a rich landscape of transport phenomena arising from the interplay between Fabry-Perot cavity modes and Fano interference. We demonstrate that the inter-impurity distance acts as a critical control parameter, allowing for the formation of Bound States in the Continuum (BICs). Furthermore, we identify Quasi-BICs, extremely narrow resonances with finite lifetimes, that can be dynamically tuned by the drive amplitude. We show that these states enable a robust coherent trapping mechanism, allowing the system to switch from perfect transparency or reflection to strong localization with giant Wigner time delays. Our results suggest possible applications for tunable delay lines and quantum memories, with feasible experimental realizations in the context of cold atoms.
summary: This work uses Floquet engineering to study quantum transport through two driven impurities in a 1D channel. By mapping the time-dependent problem to a multi-channel scattering model, the authors reveal that the system's transport properties are governed by the interplay of Fabry-Pérot cavity modes and Fano interference. This allows for the active control of quantum states, enabling programmable delay lines and robust coherent trapping mechanisms.
main_problem: The paper investigates how periodically driving two impurities in a 1D channel modifies quantum transport, leading to complex interference and trapping phenomena.
main_result: The system exhibits tunable transport regimes, allowing switching between perfect transparency/reflection and strong localization via Quasi-Bound States (Quasi-BICs) with giant Wigner time delays.
method: The time-dependent Hamiltonian is mapped onto a time-independent, multi-channel scattering problem using Floquet theory.
model_or_system: The model is a mesoscopic 1D channel containing two impurities, whose potentials are periodically driven by an external field. The analysis uses the Floquet formalism to solve the resulting multi-channel scattering problem.
key_observables: Reflection and transmission amplitudes, Bound States in the Continuum (BICs), Quasi-BICs, and Wigner time delays.
important_parameters:
Inter-impurity distance ($l$), drive amplitude ($\gamma$), and static potentials ($
u$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping the time-dependent problem to a stationary multi-channel scattering problem using Floquet theory, though approximations are noted for high-order channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the 1D system setup, the multi-channel scattering diagram, and spectral shapes showing Fano resonance profiles (dip-peak vs. peak-dip) and transmission probability vs. energy.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing the single-impurity case (showing Fano resonances) to the two-impurity system, which is treated as a dynamical Fabry-Pérot cavity, culminating in the demonstration of tunable trapping and delay lines.
why_interesting: The combination of Floquet engineering, cavity physics, and resonance phenomena (Fano/BIC) makes this highly relevant for AMO physicists working on engineered quantum systems, especially those involving cold atoms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sketch of the one-dimensional system with two im- purities. The arrow indicates the incoming particle with mo- mentum k0, while the two vertical red dashed lines represent the localized, periodically driven impurities.

FIG. 2. Sketch of the multi-channel setup arising from the Floquet mapping of the time-dependent Hamiltonian H(t) onto the extended Hilbert space. The coupling γ1 = γ2 = γ correspond to the drive amplitudes of the time-dependent model and ν1 = ν2 = ν are the amplitudes of the static poten- tial. The incoming plane wave eik0x , along with the possible reflection and transmission pathways, are depicted by arrows. The reflection and transmission amplitudes are denoted by r(′) n and t(′) n , respectively.

FIG. 3. Normalized Fano profile, F(ϵ) = T(ϵ)/(1 + q2), with T given by Eq. (20). For q > 0, the profile exhibits a dip-peak structure, while for q < 0 it shows a peak-dip structure. The Lorentzian limits correspond to q →±∞(peak) and q = 0 (dip).

FIG. 4. Transport properties of a single periodically driven impurity . Transmission probability as a function of incident energy ˜E and driving amplitude ˜γ are displayed for (a) the case ˜ν = 0 and (b) with an additional static potential (˜ν = 0.7). For both numerical solutions 41 channels have been taken into account. Panel (c) shows the energy profiles of the transmission probability at fixed drive amplitude (˜γ = 0.5). The solid curves represent the results obtained within the 2-channel approximation, while the dotted curves correspond to the 41-channel case. For both ˜ν = 0 and ˜ν = 0.7, the vertical dotted lines mark the theoretical predictions derived from Eq. (25) for ˜ν = 0 and...

FIG. 5. Transmission probability T for the symmetric two-impurity system as a function of energy of the incoming particle ˜E and driving amplitude ˜γ, with an inter-impurity distance ˜l = 7.07. (a) Case without static potential (˜ν = 0). The solid vertical lines indicate the theoretical positions of the Fabry-Pérot cavity modes [Eq. (30)] as indicated in the legend in panel (b). The squared dashed rectangles represent the regions of the transmisison spectrum I, II, III and IV characterized by different regimes. (b) Case with static potential (˜ν = 0.7).
